title: Sovereign Ratings, Default Risk and Markets: The Moody's Downgrade Aftermath!
gist: Damodaran uses Moody's May 16, 2025 downgrade of the US from Aaa to Aa1 (following S&P in 2011 and Fitch in 2023) to explain how sovereign ratings and CDS spreads work, why the market reaction was muted, and to walk through the mechanical (but small) impact on US riskfree rates and equity risk premiums.

themes.theme: History and consequences of sovereign default

insights.claim: Governments increasingly choose to default on local-currency debt even though they could print money to avoid it, because they fear the inflation consequences of printing more than the consequences of default.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Local-currency default is a policy choice, not a technical necessity, so investors should watch political willingness to inflate away debt as a separate risk factor from a country's technical ability to pay.
insights.locator: Sovereign Defaults: A History

insights.claim: Sovereign default causes real GDP to drop between 0.5% and 2%, with the bulk of the decline concentrated in the first year after default and the effect fading quickly thereafter.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The growth hit from a default is real but short-lived, useful for sizing macro risk when a country nears default rather than assuming permanent damage.
insights.locator: Default has a negative impact on the economy

insights.claim: Countries that have defaulted since 1970 carry credit ratings one to two notches lower and borrowing costs 0.5% to 1% higher than otherwise similar countries that never defaulted, though this penalty fades over time.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A default leaves a measurable but temporary borrowing-cost scar, useful for pricing recovery-story sovereign bonds after a restructuring.
insights.locator: Default does affect a country's long-term sovereign rating

insights.claim: Sovereign default triggers trade retaliation, with one study finding an 8% drop in bilateral trade after default that persists for up to 15 years, hitting export-oriented industries hardest.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Export-heavy sectors in a defaulting country are a specific pressure point to watch or short in a default scenario, given the multi-year trade drag.
insights.quote: a drop of 8% in bilateral trade after default, with the effects lasting for up to 15 years
insights.locator: Sovereign default can cause trade retaliation

insights.claim: A study of 149 countries from 1975-2000 found the probability of a banking crisis is 14% in countries that have defaulted versus roughly 3% otherwise, an eleven percentage-point increase.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Default risk should be priced into domestic bank credit exposure, not just sovereign bonds, since defaults materially raise banking-crisis odds.
insights.locator: Sovereign default can make banking systems more fragile

insights.claim: Currency devaluations, which often accompany sovereign default, raise the probability of a change in top political leadership by 45% and the probability of a change in the finance minister or central bank head by 64%, based on a study of devaluations from 1971-2003.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A currency crisis is a leading indicator of near-term political and policy-leadership turnover, relevant for handicapping EM political risk.
insights.locator: Sovereign default also increases the likelihood of political change

themes.theme: Sovereign ratings: growth, structure and biases

insights.claim: Moody's sovereign ratings coverage grew from about 13 countries (mostly Aaa) in the early 1980s to 143 countries by 2024, covering 75% of emerging markets and nearly every developed market.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The sovereign ratings universe is now broad and dense enough to be a usable global risk map, not just a developed-market club.
insights.locator: Moody's has been rating corporate bonds since 1919

insights.claim: The number of Aaa-rated countries was identical (thirteen) in 1985 and in 2025, even though the total number of Moody's-rated countries grew more than tenfold over the same period, meaning ratings agencies have effectively raised the bar for the top rating even as they lowered the bar for coverage.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Aaa scarcity is structural, not a one-off reaction to the US downgrade, so investors should expect the Aaa club to keep shrinking as more countries are added at the bottom.
insights.quote: the number of Aaa rated countries was the same in 1985, when there were thirteen rated countries, as in 2025, when there were 143 rated countries
insights.locator: Not only have ratings agencies become more active

insights.claim: In the last two decades at least five major sovereigns - Japan, the UK, France, and now the US - have lost their Aaa rating.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The US downgrade is part of a broader multi-decade trend of developed-market Aaa erosion, not an isolated US-specific event.
insights.locator: In the last two decades, at least five sovereigns

insights.claim: Sovereign ratings exhibit herd behavior: when one agency lowers or raises a rating, the others tend to follow, which undermines the value of having three nominally independent ratings agencies.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't treat three agency ratings as three independent signals - a downgrade by one raises the odds the others follow, so positioning ahead of a second/third downgrade can be informed by the first.
insights.locator: There is herd behavior

insights.claim: Sovereign ratings changes tend to be too little, too late - agencies are slow to update ratings, so the changes often arrive after a crisis has already done its damage rather than protecting investors ahead of it.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Ratings should be treated as lagging confirmation, not an early warning system; investors need faster proxies like CDS spreads for timely signal.
insights.locator: Too little, too late

insights.claim: Ratings agencies are perceived as too lenient on North America and Europe and too strict on Asia, Latin America and Africa for comparable risk profiles, a regional bias widely believed among investors in those regions.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: If regional bias is real, EM sovereign risk may be systematically overstated relative to developed markets, implying possible relative value in EM sovereign credit.
insights.locator: Regional biases

insights.claim: Moody's default data from 1983-2024 shows a 24% default rate for speculative-grade sovereign debt (Baa2 and below) versus just 1.8% for investment-grade sovereign debt (Aaa to Baa1), confirming ratings are strongly correlated with actual default risk despite their timing flaws.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Despite lag and bias criticisms, the investment-grade/speculative-grade line is still the single most powerful predictor of sovereign default and remains a valid screen.
insights.quote: a default rate of 24% for  speculative grade sovereign debt (Baa2 and below) as opposed to 1.8% for investment grade (Aaa to Baa1) sovereign debt
insights.locator: Default rates rise as sovereign ratings decline

themes.theme: Sovereign CDS as a faster read on default risk

insights.claim: The sovereign CDS market has expanded over the last two decades to cover about 80 countries as of the start of 2025, offering a more timely, market-priced alternative to slow-moving agency ratings, despite counterparty-risk and default-definition issues.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Sovereign CDS spreads are the more actionable, real-time gauge of default risk versus waiting on rating agency action.
insights.locator: the development of the sovereign CDS market

insights.claim: Even among Aaa-rated countries at the start of 2025, the CDS market already priced meaningful risk differences: Scandinavia, Switzerland, Australia and New Zealand traded at 10-year CDS spreads of 0.20% or below, while the US, Germany and Canada - also Aaa at the time - traded wider, showing the market saw the US as a weaker Aaa well before Moody's acted.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Market-based default pricing had already flagged the US as second-tier among Aaa credits, so the eventual downgrade was confirmation rather than new information.
insights.locator: the safest Aaa-rated countries (Scandinavia, Switzerland, Australia and New Zealand)

insights.claim: The US sovereign CDS spread rose from 0.41% at the start of 2025 to 0.62% in late April amid tariff and trade-war shocks, eased back somewhat by May, and the Moody's downgrade itself had almost no additional effect on the spread.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Trade-policy shocks moved US default-risk pricing far more than the ratings downgrade did - watch tariff escalation, not rating headlines, for the next leg of US sovereign risk repricing.
insights.quote: the Moody's downgrade having almost no effect on the US sovereign CDS spread
insights.locator: During 2025, the market shocks from tariff and trade wars

themes.theme: Why Moody's downgraded the US now

insights.claim: US total federal debt rose from about 80% of GDP in 2008 to more than 120% in 2024, and Damodaran argues it is disingenuous to keep blaming the 2008 banking crisis and 2020 COVID bailouts for today's level since the debt never came back down once those crises passed.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The structural, not cyclical, nature of the debt buildup means fiscal repair requires policy choices, not just the passage of time or the next growth cycle.
insights.quote: total federal debt rising from 80% of GDP in 2008  to more than 120% in 2024
insights.locator: The ramping up of US debt since 2008

insights.claim: Debt-to-GDP is an imperfect proxy for sovereign risk because many countries with the highest debt-to-GDP ratios (some Aaa-rated) are considered safe, while many countries with low debt-to-GDP ratios are considered risky, per 2023 IMF data cited in the piece.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Don't rely on debt/GDP alone to rank sovereign risk across countries - institutional and currency factors matter more than the headline leverage ratio.
insights.locator: The problem with the debt-to-GDP measure

insights.claim: Since US debt-to-GDP had been rising for years before 2025, the real trigger for Moody's timing was political - a loss of confidence that US institutions remain willing to discipline fiscal and monetary policy, not a sudden change in the debt math itself.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Future rating and market actions on the US are more likely to be triggered by political dysfunction (debt-ceiling brinkmanship, reconciliation bills, gridlock) than by any single fiscal data print.
insights.quote: The Moody's downgrade seems to me a signal that those days might be winding down, and that the United States, like the rest of the world, will face more accountability for lack of discipline in its fiscal and monetary policy.
insights.locator: For decades now, the US has enjoyed special status

insights.claim: Moody's put the US on negative outlook in November 2023, roughly 18 months before the actual May 2025 downgrade, so the eventual action was well telegraphed rather than a surprise.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A negative-outlook flag from a rating agency is a usable early signal for an eventual downgrade roughly a year or more out.
insights.locator: Moody's changed its outlook for the US to negative in November 2023

themes.theme: Muted market reaction to the downgrade

insights.claim: US equities actually rose on Monday May 19, the first trading day after the Friday May 16 downgrade, and only lost ground gradually over the following days rather than selling off immediately.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Fears of an immediate downgrade-driven selloff proved unfounded, illustrating how much of a well-telegraphed rating action is pre-priced by markets.
insights.locator: Market Reaction

insights.claim: Treasury yields rose in the days right after the downgrade, but the move was confounded by a reconciliation bill working through the House that could add to future deficits, and by May 29 nearly all of the downgrade-linked rate move had faded back to where yields started the year.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: It's hard to isolate a clean 'downgrade effect' on yields because it overlapped with fiscal-bill news; the rating action alone did not durably move rates.
insights.locator: While rates did go up in the the first few days

insights.claim: The muted market reaction to the US downgrade is explained by three factors: no surprise (S&P and Fitch had already downgraded and Moody's had been on negative outlook since Nov 2023), the dominance of private capital over government borrowing in funding the US economy, and the small default-spread gap between Aaa and Aa1 ratings.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Use this three-factor checklist to gauge whether any future sovereign downgrade elsewhere is likely to move markets, or is already priced in.
insights.locator: Lack of surprise effect

insights.claim: Austria and Finland, both rated Aa1 in May 2025, had ten-year euro bonds trading only about 0.15-0.20% over Germany's Aaa-rated ten-year euro bond, illustrating how small the actual default-spread gap is between an Aaa and an Aa1 rating.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The Aaa-to-Aa1 downgrade is financially almost meaningless in spread terms - roughly 15-20bps - reinforcing that the US downgrade was more reputational than economic.
insights.locator: Ratings change

themes.theme: Valuation mechanics: riskfree rate and equity risk premium

insights.claim: Once a government is no longer top-rated, the correct riskfree rate is not the raw government bond yield but that yield minus an implied default spread: Riskfree rate = Government Bond rate - Default spread for the government.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any analyst still plugging the raw 10-year Treasury yield into a DCF as a pure riskfree rate is implicitly overstating the true riskfree component and needs to net out the sovereign default spread.
insights.locator: Risk free rate = Government Bond rate

insights.claim: Applying the rating-implied default spread to the US: a 4.41% ten-year Treasury rate on May 30, 2025 minus a 0.40% default spread for the Aa1 rating gives an implied US-dollar riskfree rate of 4.01%, about 40 basis points below the headline Treasury yield.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Valuation models using the US 10-year yield directly are now overstating the riskfree rate by roughly 40bps according to this framework, which should be netted into a higher equity risk premium instead.
insights.quote: Riskfree rate in US dollars on May 30, 2025 = US 10-year treasury rate - Aa1 default spread = 4.41% - 0.40% = 4.01%
insights.locator: US 10-year treasury bond rate on May 30, 2025

insights.claim: Damodaran argues the more realistic interpretation is not that a default spread suddenly appeared in Treasury yields on May 16, but that US Treasury markets have been implicitly carrying a default spread for years - the downgrade just makes it explicit.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The US Treasury 'riskfree' rate has arguably been mispriced as default-free for years; the downgrade is a labeling correction rather than a new risk being introduced.
insights.quote: default spread embedded in them for years, and that we are not making it explicit.
insights.locator: This adjustment yields a riskfree rate of 4.01%

insights.claim: Because the US is no longer the Aaa benchmark, Damodaran will now net the adjusted, lower US-dollar riskfree rate out of the S&P 500 earnings yield when computing the US equity risk premium, and will for the first time assign Aaa-rated countries like Canada, Germany, Australia and Singapore an equity risk premium below the US's own.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect equity risk premiums for still-Aaa developed markets to be re-benchmarked below the US ERP going forward, which could nudge relative cost-of-equity and valuation multiples between the US and those markets.
insights.locator: With the ratings downgrade, I will have to modify this process

insights.claim: Recomputing cost of equity for the S&P 500 with the downgrade factored in leaves the expected return for the average stock unchanged at 8.64% as of May 30, 2025 - the composition just shifts to a lower riskfree rate (4.01% vs 4.41%) and a higher equity risk premium (4.63% vs 4.23%), with riskier stocks seeing slightly higher costs of equity and safer stocks slightly lower, changes too small for investors to notice.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The downgrade is a valuation-mechanics footnote, not a reason to reprice US equities broadly - the net effect on discount rates is immaterial for most portfolios.
insights.quote: the expected return for the average stock remains at 8.64%
insights.locator: the expected return on the S&P 500 as of May 30, 2025

insights.claim: For practical purposes, analysts can continue using the US 10-year Treasury rate as the riskfree rate and conventional forward-looking equity risk premiums, since the downgrade's mechanical adjustment nets out to almost nothing.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: No need to overhaul standard US valuation practice because of the downgrade - the conventional shortcut still holds within a rounding error.
insights.locator: staying with the conventional approach

insights.claim: Damodaran frames the Moody's downgrade as mostly symbolic financially but significant as a signal that the era of 'US exceptionalism' - where the US could run fiscal and economic policies that would trigger market punishment elsewhere, especially in emerging markets, without the same consequences - is coming to an end.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should expect the US to increasingly face the same market discipline (yield premia, currency pressure) historically reserved for emerging markets when running large deficits.
insights.locator: the Moody's action does carry symbolic weight

insights.claim: Damodaran expects the normalization of US fiscal accountability to be healthy in the long run for both the US and the rest of the world, but warns it will come with short-term pain.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Position for episodic volatility around US fiscal news (debt ceiling, deficit bills) even while the long-run adjustment is constructive.
insights.quote: That is healthy, in the long term, for both the United States and the rest of the world, but it will come with short term pain.
insights.locator: Conclusion
